speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Date is October 17, 1996. Survivor is Jacob Wiener, born Zwienicki. The interviewer is Dina Cohen. The interview is in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. And the interview will be in English. My name is Dina Cohen. Today is October 17, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Jacob Wiener, born Zwienicki, in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. The interview will be in English. Could you please state your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob G. Wiener, W-I-E-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zwienicki, Z-W-I-E-N-I-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what does the G stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a middle name, Gerd, G-E-R-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date, the day you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bremen, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Bremen, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-E-M-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe Bremen? What kind of a town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a middle-sized town. It was one of the two ports which lead from Germany to the North Sea. Hamburg is the other one. And it's an-- inland port. And many ships went from there to the United States into other countries. It was a middle-sized town, about 360,000 people. And it was an independent city. In Germany, they had different states. This was called a Hansestadt from the Middle Ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Bremen, at least I was born in Bremen. And in our house, we had a house. I lived with my father, my mother, I, and then I had besides this, two brothers and a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names and-- and your siblings' names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Josef, J-O-S-E-F. And my mother's name was Selma, S-E-L-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me was born my brother by the name of Benno, B-E-N-N-O. Then came a sister, by name of Liesel, L-I-E-S-E-L. And the youngest brother, Alfred, A-L-F-R-E-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have extended family nearby, grandparents or aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And the closest family nearby was in Hamberg, which was about 60 kilometers away from Bremen. And these were my-- my mother's relatives mainly. My father's relatives I never saw except once, when his mother came to Germany in 1936. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was-- was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother was more religious than my father. But we kept a kosher household. And I went to synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a-- a large Jewish community in Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was very small. Of the 360,000 people there, there may only be 1,400 Jews. But these Jews, mostly in Bremen, they came from-- from America and Poland and different East European countries. And they were waiting there to go to America, most of them. So they were stranded there usually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have a lot of Jewish friends or non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had both. I had Jewish friends and non-Jewish friends. Since in our neighborhood where we lived in the southern part of the city, which was called the new city, on the other side of the-- of the Weser, W-E-S-E-R, that's the river which goes through Bremen. It's called the newer city, and the-- the older city on the north and the newer city on the south. There were not too many Jews living there in the newer part. But I had neighbors. Most of them were non-Jewish. But we were good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you attend school in Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I attended school in Bremen. I attended first of all, the public school, till about when I was 10 years old. And then in-- in Germany there used to be an examination which the students took when they were 10 years old, to find out whether they were going to a higher kind learning or-- learning or lower kind of learning. I passed it, and I went in what's called a realschule or the oberrealschule. That was also in the city of Bremen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that also a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you had to pay for it, partly, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things did you study there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You study a lot of things. First all of all, there were-- you study history a lot in-- in Germany. You studied a lot of history, especially before Hitler came to power. And you study mathematics, sciences, reading, writing, many things. This school where I went to, the realschule, actually the oberrealschule, there you went for six years. After six years, you could decide if you wanted to go another three years. If you went there for six years and you wanted to leave, you could join the army, and you would immediately get an officer's rank. If you wanted to stay continuously three more years, then you had to choose if you wanted to go into the language sequence, or the mathematics sequence. I went into the language sequence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in school, did you ever experience any antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always even covert antisemitism in Germany always. But it wasn't so rampant later on after the Nazis came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the Nazis came to power, were there ever any incidents of antisemitism that you were specifically involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can tell you of one instance. We-- we had a class in drawing. And then we went outside. And we were drawing things in nature, like the river, or churches, or other things like this. And one day, one of my classmates-- it was-- most of them were non-Jews you see. He said to me, why don't you go back to Jordan? I said to him, what do you mean? I was never in Jordan.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, no, all Jews have to go back there. Anyhow, they made some kind of sinister remarks. And what kinds of things did you do after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left the oberrealschule?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or, no-- while you were in school, but after the school day was completed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the afternoons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Germany, school was from 8:00 in the morning to 3:00. And then they had after-school activities. For instance, gymnastics and other things like this they had. When Hitler came to power, after school, they-- the Nazis' children had to go to a party and learn about their party. But they also learned shooting. I even learned shooting by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had-- they went to a special place. And they taught you how to handle a rifle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably about 19-- in the middle '30s, after the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: But prior to 1933, did you belong to any clubs or organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to-- we belonged to the Jewish community there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And actually, some youth groups, and in summer, we went to summer camp. But I don't think there was any particular group or organization I belonged to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to summer camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to summer camp nearly every summer. The summer vacation in the German schools were five weeks, not two months like here or three months. And during this time, I went to summer camps in-- near Hamburg, near Westphalia, which is in southern part of-- of-- of Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once I went to near Denmark to-- an island, we called Fur, because when I was 10 years old, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jewish camps or mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- these camps were usually sponsored by the Jewish Women's Organization. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and sometimes you got a part-time subsidy from them, you know, and you went there. Because we-- we weren't-- we didn't belong to the rich people. We didn't even belong to middle-class people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a typical Sabbath in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to synagogue on Friday evening, and then came home. It was a long walk. It was not nearby us. It was about a 30 minutes or 35 minutes walk. I had to cross the river, the River Weser, across a bridge. And-- because the synagogue was in the other part of the city. And when we came home, we made kiddish. And we prayed a little bit. On Saturday morning my father didn't-- wasn't so religious. He had the business open. But I went back to synagogue. In fact, in the beginning, I even went to school on the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to school because it was a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see the-- the-- the ruling in-- in the German schools at that time was six days a week. School was six days a week. And only in 1934, that was already one year after Hitler came to power, they made a law that if Jewish people go to schools, you should go to school on Saturday. They have to do everything. Before that time, the people who were more Orthodox did not have to write or carry books on Saturday. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you bar mitzvah'd?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was bar mitzvah'd in Bremen. And it was not such a big affair like they have now in the United States. It was a very small affair. But interestingly enough, I remember on my bar mitzvah, there were about 40 rabbis, I think, who came from Poland and Russia on their way to the United States. And they stopped off in Bremen. And they were like my visitors. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But then in the afternoon, we just had a little bit of party. People came to our home, not very many, but a few came. And you get only a few things when you were [INAUDIBLE], and maybe a shirt and the pants, and a pen or that sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about the Nazi rise to power in the early '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one incident in 1928. When I went with my mother in a street car, they call it here tramway. And in stormed a number of Nazis. And they said, we are going to get the Jews. So my mother was very frightened. And I thought to myself, I hope it will not come true. But it came true, you see. Because in 1928 that was the time when the Nazis got the majority in the House, in the Reichstag. So there were always Nazis there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about when Hitler was actually named Chancellor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember this happened on January 30-- it was a Monday-- 1933, in the evening. Now you don't-- didn't have at that time television. But we had a radio. And the radio, you had to scratch with a crystal. And we listened to it, and they made a lot of noise. And we were all very much upset that something bad is going to happen. But our non-Jewish neighbors, and I had a lot of friends there, they said to me, don't worry. It will pass over. We were not so sure about it. Because then, when he came to power for seven days, they did nothing else, no work. There were on a holiday. They marched in the streets. They sang music, and they sang the song-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about the people who were killed when Hitler made the putsch 10 years earlier in 1923, and they made a song which was called, which goes like this, translated from the German. 'When Jewish blood jumps off the knife, we feel twice as good.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family and you think about emigrating at that time at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, we didn't think much of emigrating. Because my mother, especially, had all her relatives in Hamburg, and she didn't want to separate from them. But as time went on, we thought more and more about it. We started writing letters to the outside world, to others, whomever we could. But the answers were always negative.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your life change after Hitler became Chancellor?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, as I said, in 1934 about a year after, I did not go to school anymore on Sabbath. Because they made this law that if you go, you have to do everything. But I still had teachers from the time, from before Hitler, who were a little bit more human and understanding. And I had a teacher who was already at that time 65 years old, close to retirement age at that time. He said to me, if you don't come on Saturday, I want to see that your-- your co-students, your-- your classmates you see will give you the work which we do on-- on Saturday. And on Saturday I will teach them Hitler's, That's the book which Hitler wrote, you see, which everyone had to read. But let me say, already very early in the beginning when Hitler came to power, I joined first a Zionist party as a youth leader. But this Zionist party was not-- was not enough Orthodox for me. So I left it. And I-- I joined the Agudat Israel, a different party at that time. It's not a party. It was a religious group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you join that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933 or '34, I think, about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in that organization, or what did that organization do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, many Jews started at that time to think about emigrating. And the Zionist organizations talked about emigrating to Israel, at that time-- Palestine, at that time. And-- but they were not very religious. And I did not want to mix into politics. See, they were mixing politics. Some of them were even Communists. And I didn't want to be there. So I left them. And left them, and about 50-60 people went out with me. And we went to the other organization, which was not politically inclined, but most religiously, working on that level.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what kinds of things did you do in that religious organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had meetings. We talked about religious subjects. We talked about the Torah. And we learned Hebrew and other things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you maintain any contact with your non-Jewish friends from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I always had contact with my non-Jewish friends. My father also. Because I did not tell you that my father had a bicycle business. And he did a very good job. He was one of the better mechanics. People came to him from all over. And he was very close to these people. They came to him, and they talked to him. And my mother, too, was very close. Because at that time, I did not tell you that. We had no typewriters. And my mother had very excellent handwriting. I still have some samples of it. So people came from far and wide, wanting their letters to be written in handwriting, longhand. And she did a very good job. So we were very close with them. But as time went on, during-- when Hitler came to power, he told all his people, break off your contacts with all Jews. And it went so far that when they saw a Jew on the other side of the street, they said, I go away. Turn this way. Don't look at him, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Because these people didn't want to have any more contact with us, you see? So things went this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's business affected by the anti-Jewish boycott in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a boycott on April 1. It was a Saturday in 1933. That was the first boycott. And I remember people said at that time, and the Nazis said at that time, they say today Jews and dogs are not allowed in the street. So-- so the thing is, maybe dogs are allowed in the street. But Jews and gypsies-- they were against the gypsies very much-- and so the dog could go in the street, but not the Jew. They were standing before the door, and they didn't let anyone in, and they had the big signs, and made a lot of noise. Yeah, that was during the boycott there. Later on, they showed their-- their enmity, I mean, against the Jews by planting a man next door to us in the business, in the bicycle business like us. This man didn't know very much about bicycles, and didn't know very much about repair. So my father taught him. And many people came to him. But actually, he sent them over to us. Because he couldn't do the work. This went on. Then they told this man, who was an member of the Nazi party, and those people who had an early number in the Nazi Party got priority. He told the the man-- they told this man to build a wall next to our house so high that our window on the second floor was three quarters covered. And he took away all of our yard, which we had there and so forth. They did this, so they showed it by doing all kinds of things. They-- they sent people to our business buying things, and later on these things were stolen, which wasn't even true. They tried to sell us stolen merchandise. We never bought that. I mean they did a lot of tricks and so forth, you see to get the Jews into prison. I know one classmate of mine went to prison. But he was suspected of being a Communist. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in the oberrealschule at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was in the oberrealschule till 1936. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At one time, in the beginning, the teachers came into the school. And in Germany there is a-- a custom, because German teachers are called officials. I don't know how it is here. So when the teacher comes in, everyone has to stand up and give a greeting. When the teacher leaves, everyone stands up and give a greeting. They have two kinds of greetings. They have what they called a Hitler greeting and an German greeting. The German greeting is like this. The Hitler greeting is like this. I didn't have to do it. But if they didn't know I was a Jew, they would beat me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the thing is [LAUGHS] one day the teacher, just a little bit after Hitler came to power, came over to me. And he said to me, it was a French teacher. He comes from Alsace-Lorraine, which is at the border between Germany and France, near Strasbourg. And he said to me, here. I have here the emblem of the Nazi Party, which is like a ring and the swastika in the middle. And it says in NASDP, NSDAP, which is the abbreviation of the Nazi, Nationalsocialistishe Deutsch Arbeiterpartei. And he said to me, I had to put it on. This is my life saver. Because it was a double ring. But don't think that I really feel this way. He said to me. And many times in our business, former people who belong to Communist party came to us, and they talked to us. And they said to me, they said to my father, consider me a burnt pancake. Do you know what a burnt pancake is? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's brown from the outside and red from the inside. So they put on a Nazi uniform, and they still were Communists, because they had burnt inside. Was your father political at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was not political-- he was not political in any sense. He never wanted to be political. Because, he told me that in 1929, when they had the inflation, the Germans wanted to send-- wanted to sell German citizenship for 50 marks, very cheap. And they came again and again, and said, why don't you buy it? My father said, I don't want to buy any kind of political-- any kind of political things. And besides this, Germany is known for antisemitism, probably the root of antisemitism. And we was right, because 10 years later, they took away all citizenships, even from those who were born in Germany. And if you're born in Germany, you don't become a German citizen by that anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, your father was a citizen of what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Father was not a citizen at all. Because he was born in Russia, in Ukraine. And near Kiev, Kharkov. And when he came over, he was a Russian citizen. However, when the Russian empire-- call it-- became Communist, Soviet-- Soviet-- Socialist Republic in 1922, '23, the Lenin, at that time, made a law that whoever wants to remain a Russian citizen must come here within three months, for a short period of time. But my father didn't want to go back. And so all those who didn't go back, became stateless. So we were stateless, staatenlos. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your life change when the Nuremberg laws were passed? in '35?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nuremberg laws were passed in 1935, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it didn't change too much from what it was before. Because we knew already what was going to happen. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, the antisemitism got worse. And people started to openly accuse accuse the Jews and-- kept away from them much more than ever before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop now.
